'''Alan Christopher Hunte'''  (born 11 July 1970) is an English former professional [[rugby league]] and [[rugby union]] footballer who played between 1989 and 2003. He played rugby league (RL) at representative level for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], and at club level for [[Wakefield Trinity]], [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], [[Hull FC]], [[Warrington Wolves]] and [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] as a [[Rugby league positions#The three-quarters|three-quarter]],<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/> and club level rugby union (RU) for [[Pontypridd RFC]].

==Background==
Alan Hunte was born in [[Wakefield]], [[West Riding of Yorkshire]], England. His father, Michael, played [[rugby league]] for [[Wakefield Trinity]] and [[Dewsbury Rams|Dewsbury]].<ref>{{cite web |title=Rugby League Heroes: Michael Hunte |website=Total Rugby League |url=https://www.totalrl.com/rugby-league-heroes-michael-hunte/ |access-date=24 January 2026 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220218120701/https://www.totalrl.com/rugby-league-heroes-michael-hunte/ |archive-date=18 February 2022 |date=18 February 2022}}</ref>

==Playing career==
Hunte started his [[rugby league]] career with Eastmoor, and was selected for the [[British Amateur Rugby League Association|BARLA]] Young Lions tour of Australia in 1989.<ref>{{cite news |title=Young Lions beaten again |work=The Guardian |date=29 July 1989 |location=London |page=19 |id={{ProQuest|186885909}}}}</ref> He signed amateur forms with [[Wakefield Trinity]] during the [[1988–89 Rugby Football League season|1988–89 season]], making his debut for the club in January 1989, but chose to sign for [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]] two months later.<ref>{{cite news |title=Test plans for Wembley |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0004985/19890304/043/0043 |work=Manchester Evening News Pink |date=4 March 1989 |page=11 |via=[[British Newspaper Archive]] |url-access=subscription}}</ref> He scored two tries on his debut for St Helens on 1 March 1989 in a 58–12 win against [[Oldham R.L.F.C.|Oldham]].<ref>{{cite news |last1=Fitzpatrick |first1=Paul |title=Hunte steals triumph from Aussie masters |work=The Guardian |date=2 March 1989 |location=London |page=15 |id={{ProQuest|187029101}}}}</ref> He was not eligible to play for Saints in the [[1989 Challenge Cup]] final due to being cup-tied, having played for Wakefield in an earlier round of the cup.<ref>{{cite web |title=Rugby League Heroes: Alan Hunte |url=https://www.totalrl.com/rugby-league-heroes-alan-hunte/ |website=Total Rugby League |access-date=13 May 2023 |date=1 March 2022}}</ref>

Hunte played on the {{rlp|wg}} in [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]' 4–5 defeat by [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] in the [[1992 Lancashire Cup]] Final during the [[1992–93 Rugby Football League season|1992–93 season]] at [[Knowsley Road]], [[St Helens, Merseyside|St. Helens]], on Sunday 18 October 1992.<ref name="1992-1993 Lancashire Cup Final">{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2745|title=1992–1993 Lancashire Cup Final|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com|date=31 December 2014|access-date=1 January 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180713170348/http://wigan.rlfans.com/news.php?readmore=2745|archive-date=13 July 2018|url-status=dead}}</ref>

In 1996, Hunte was a substitute in their [[1996 Challenge Cup]] final victory over [[Bradford Bulls]].

Hunte, together with [[Anthony Sullivan (rugby)|Anthony Sullivan]] was the [[1997 St Helens season]]'s top [[Try (rugby)|try]] scorer.

In November 1997, [[Hull F.C.|Hull]] signed Hunte, along with his St Helens teammates [[Steve Prescott]] and [[Simon Booth (English rugby league)|Simon Booth]], for a combined fee of £350,000.<ref>{{cite web |title=Rugby League: Hull sign up Hunte, Prescott and Booth |url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-hull-sign-up-hunte-prescott-and-booth-1296411.html |website=The Independent |access-date=13 May 2023 |date=26 November 1997}}</ref>

Hunte later moved to [[Warrington Wolves]] and [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]].

Hunte also switched codes to [[Rugby Union]], joining [[Pontypridd RFC]] in 2000 in a blaze of publicity. Hunte's career at Pontypridd was short lived, however, as he struggled to come to grips with the vagaries of the Union code.<ref name="Profile at ponty.net">{{cite web|url=http://www.ponty.net/hunte-alan|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071107130740/http://www.ponty.net/hunte-alan|url-status=dead|archive-date=7 November 2007|title=Profile at ponty.net|publisher=ponty.net|date=31 December 2017|access-date=1 January 2018}}</ref>

===International career===
Hunte was selected to go on the [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australia and New Zealand]], and would play for the Lions in the [[1992 Rugby League World Cup final]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley]] in October, though it was his dropped ball which led to débuting [[Australian Kangaroos|Australian]] {{rlp|ce}} [[Steve Renouf]] scoring the only (and winning) try of the match.

In the 1997 post season, Hunte was selected to play for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]] on the {{rlp|wg}} in all three matches of the [[Super League Test series]] against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]]. His speed was shown in the third test when he ran down Aussie speedster [[Andrew Ettingshausen]] over a 70m run after giving him a 10m start.

==Coaching career==
Hunte worked for 13 years within the coaching setup at [[Salford Red Devils]] as Head of Youth Development.<ref>{{cite web |title=Alan Hunte leaves Salford as club seek ‘fresh approach’ to academy |url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2015/may/15/alan-hunte-leaves-salford-academy |website=The Guardian |access-date=24 January 2026 |date=15 May 2015}}</ref>

He took over as caretaker head coach in 2013 when [[Phil Veivers]] was sacked.
